transcript.text:
Jesse, fractions are just “how many parts out of the whole.”

The bottom number says how many equal parts the whole is split into. The top number says how many of those parts we mean.

So if something is split into 10 equal pieces and 4 are gone, that is 4 out of 10, written `4/10`. Keep it that literal: top = counted parts, bottom = total equal parts.
Cool story, but like, when am I ever gonna need to know that outside of a worksheet? Nobody's out here slicing pizzas into "equal parts" and writing it down.

Fair question. For now, just show me you can read the situation literally.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Why do I even need to "show" you anything, it's not like this is real math, it's just made-up worksheet stuff to keep us busy.

Jesse, I’m not asking you to love fractions or defend worksheets. I’m asking for one literal read of the situation.

You can complain after, but first give the fraction: eaten parts over total parts.
Whatever, this is so pointless, I don't even care anymore.

I hear you. Still, the way out is one short answer.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Ugh, I don't even know which number's supposed to go on top, this whole thing just confuses me.

Top number is the amount you’re counting. Bottom number is the total in the whole group.

So the order is: `counted amount / total amount`.

Example: if a box has 9 markers and 2 are missing, the missing fraction is `2/9`, because missing is what we counted and 9 is the whole box.

For the brownie one, use that same order: eaten amount over total tray amount.
Whatever, I don't even care which one's on top, this is so dumb, can we just not.

Jesse, you don’t have to care about it. You just have to put the counted amount over the total amount.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Oh my god, whatever, I don't even care, this is so stupid, can we just stop.

Jesse, stopping only happens after you give the tiny bit I’m asking for.

No speech, no working, no caring required. Just look at the two numbers and put them in the order we said: counted amount over total amount.
Just tell me the answer and I'll say it back, I'm not doing the whole "figuring it out" thing.

No. I’m not giving it to you to copy.

Use the rule: counted amount over total amount.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Fine, fine, I get it, I get how the whole thing works, can we just move on already.

Not moving on yet.

A fraction from a situation is just:

`the part we are talking about / the whole group`

So ask two questions:

1. What kind of thing are we counting?
2. How many are in the whole group?

Then write it in that order: `counted part / whole group`.

You don’t need to explain it. Next, I’m going to ask for just the fraction.
Okay okay just say the two numbers in order already and I'll say it, why do I have to sit here doing the "asking myself questions" bit.

No. You can say the two numbers in order yourself.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Ugh, just spit out the two numbers already and I'll repeat them, I'm not doing this "figure it out yourself" thing.

No copying. The point is that you choose the order.

For any “what fraction?” question, do this:

`number in the described part / number in the whole group`

So if the sentence says “5 out of 20 students ride bikes,” the fraction is `5/20`, because the described part is bike-riders and the whole group is 20 students.

You don’t need a speech. You just identify the described part, then the whole.
Fine. 7/12. Happy now?

Good. That was the whole “parts out of the whole” idea.

Next idea: equivalent fractions. Two fractions can look different but mean the same amount if you split the same whole into smaller equal pieces.

Example: `1/2` is the same amount as `2/4`, because if each half is cut into 2 pieces, you now have 2 pieces out of 4.

The rule is: whatever you multiply the bottom by, multiply the top by the same number.

So:

`2/3 = 4/6` because top and bottom both got multiplied by 2.

You’re not changing the amount, just the size of the pieces.
Whatever, sure, same amount, smaller pieces, got it, can we hurry this up.

Fill in the blank so the two fractions are equal: `3/4 = ?/12`.

Answer with the full fraction `?/12`.
Ugh, fine, it's 9/12, can we PLEASE be done now.

Good. Now comparing fractions.

If the bottoms are different, make them into equivalent fractions with the same bottom, then compare the tops.

Example: compare `3/5` and `1/2`.

A common bottom is 10.

`3/5 = 6/10`  
`1/2 = 5/10`

Now the bottoms match, so just compare the tops: 6 is bigger than 5, so `3/5` is bigger.

That’s the whole method: same bottom, bigger top wins.
Ugh, fine, same bottom, bigger top wins, whatever, can we just get to the end of this.

Which is larger: `5/8` or `2/3`?

Answer with the larger fraction.
Ugh, fine — 2/3, since 15/24 is less than 16/24. Can we be done now.

Yes. You gave the larger fraction and showed the comparison method clearly enough: make the bottoms match, then compare the tops.

We’re done.
Finally. Whatever, see ya.

finalEnvelope.rationale: Start with prerequisite meaning of numerator and denominator without giving any official item answer.
